title: Cantwell, Moran Introduce Bill to Boost AI Education
source: U.S. Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation

draft.headline: U.S. Congress Moves to Train 1M+ AI Workers by 2028 Through Landmark Education Act
draft.subheadline: Bipartisan NSF AI Education Act targets K-12 guidance, community college centers of excellence, and agriculture sector AI training
draft.html:
The United States is making its boldest move yet on AI education policy. In March 2026, Senators Maria Cantwell (D-Wash.) and Jerry Moran (R-Kan.) introduced the bipartisan NSF AI Education Act of 2026, a comprehensive initiative to expand artificial intelligence education across the entire spectrum of American education—from kindergarten through professional development. The bill aims to train over 1 million workers in AI by 2028, ensuring that the U.S. workforce can compete globally and maintain American technological leadership.

A National Strategy for AI Literacy and Workforce Development

The NSF AI Education Act represents more than a legislative proposal—it is a strategic recognition that AI education is no longer optional for national competitiveness. The bill authorizes the National Science Foundation (NSF) to award undergraduate and graduate scholarships for students pursuing any aspect of artificial intelligence: its development, deployment, integration, and application. More significantly, it establishes targeted scholarships for future educators, agricultural professionals, and workers in advanced manufacturing.

The scale of the initiative is ambitious. The goal of training 1 million or more workers on AI in the United States by 2028 reflects the urgency with which Congress views the AI workforce gap. Senator Cantwell's statement—"Demand for AI expertise is already high and will continue to grow, and we must meet that demand if we are to out-compete China and make the American AI tech stack the global standard"—directly frames AI education as a matter of national strategic competition. This is not merely about corporate innovation; it is about maintaining America's technological dominance in an era where AI is reshaping every sector of the economy.

The bill also addresses a critical gap: K-12 AI education. The NSF is tasked with collaborating with educators and academics to create publicly available guidance for introducing AI skills and understanding in American classrooms from kindergarten through high school. This emphasis on early education is strategic. Rather than waiting until college to teach AI, the U.S. approach recognizes that foundational AI literacy must begin in elementary school, just as mathematics and reading proficiency do today.

Community Colleges as Centers of AI Excellence

A distinctive feature of the NSF AI Education Act is its emphasis on community colleges. The bill calls for establishing at least five 'Centers of AI Excellence' at community colleges and vocational schools across the country. This is a deliberate choice to democratize AI education beyond four-year universities. Community colleges serve nearly 10 million students annually in the U.S., including many first-generation college students, adults returning to education, and workers seeking career transitions. By placing AI Excellence Centers in community colleges, the legislation signals that AI education should not be confined to elite universities.

These centers would focus on teaching AI in practical contexts: manufacturing, agriculture, healthcare, and other fields where American workers operate. The approach mirrors best practices already being developed in leading institutions and scales them nationally. More importantly, it recognizes that AI expertise is not monolithic. An agricultural AI specialist needs different knowledge than a manufacturing AI expert, and community colleges are positioned to provide this sector-specific training.

The bill also leverages regional innovation hubs created by the CHIPS & Science Act, creating a network effect. Rather than creating isolated centers, the legislation coordinates AI education infrastructure with existing regional technology development initiatives. This networked approach increases the likelihood of sustained investment and regional economic development.

Agriculture and Rural Communities: AI for a Changing Sector

One of the most innovative aspects of the NSF AI Education Act is its focus on agricultural AI. The bill authorizes grants for AI research, development, and training in agriculture through Land-Grant Universities and the Cooperative Extension Service. This reflects a clear policy priority: ensuring that rural communities and agricultural workers do not fall behind in the AI revolution.

American agriculture is rapidly transforming through precision farming, autonomous equipment, and data-driven crop management. Without accessible AI education, rural farmers and agricultural workers risk becoming dependent on external expertise for technologies that increasingly define farm productivity. By placing agricultural AI education within Land-Grant Universities and through the Extension Service—networks with deep rural roots—the bill ensures that AI training reaches farmers and agricultural communities directly.

This reflects broader congressional concern about regional disparities in technology access. As AI reshapes the economy, there is bipartisan recognition that failure to educate all regions would deepen inequality and weaken American competitiveness in vital sectors like agriculture and advanced manufacturing.

Addressing Inequality and Worker Displacement

A critical element of the bill is its explicit commitment to equitable AI education. The legislation mandates that the AI education and training framework does not displace existing workers—particularly teachers—and actively seeks to reach underrepresented populations including women and rural residents. This is not rhetorical; it reflects lessons learned from previous technological transitions where education policy failed to manage disruption equitably.

The bill recognizes that AI education cannot be merely additive. If current workers—especially educators themselves—are to transition into AI-augmented roles rather than be displaced, the policy must explicitly support them. By providing professional development fellowships for current educators and STEM workers, the bill acknowledges that the education system itself must transform, not just the curriculum it delivers.

The emphasis on reaching women and rural populations reflects demographic realities: women remain underrepresented in technology fields, and rural residents have historically lower access to advanced tech training. These populations are not afterthoughts in the legislation; they are explicitly recognized as strategic priorities for building a diverse AI workforce.

The NSF AI Education Act is not just about technical training. It is a statement that the United States recognizes AI as a defining technology for the 21st-century workforce and is willing to invest federal resources to ensure that opportunity for AI skills reaches all Americans—from rural farmers to inner-city students to career changers in community colleges.

The Global AI Education Race Heats Up

The timing of the NSF AI Education Act is significant. It arrives as other major powers—China, European nations, South Korea—are rapidly scaling their own AI education initiatives. The explicit goal of training 1 million workers by 2028 and the statement that the U.S. must "out-compete China" frame this legislation as part of a broader strategic competition for technological leadership.

However, the American approach differs from its competitors in important ways. Rather than centralizing AI education policy, the U.S. model distributes it across multiple institutions: the NSF, community colleges, Land-Grant Universities, private sector partners, and K-12 systems. This reflects American federalism and competitive market dynamics but also creates the risk of fragmentation. The success of the NSF AI Education Act will depend partly on how well these distributed efforts coordinate.

The bill also represents a bipartisan consensus on AI education policy—rare in today's polarized political environment. The fact that both Democrats and Republicans championed the legislation suggests that AI workforce development has transcended partisan divides and is now understood as a shared national interest. If the bill passes and is effectively implemented, it could serve as a model for how democracies democratize AI education at scale.
